title: W02.Deck.fins3646
body:
FINS3646: Toolkit for Finance
Week 2: Lecture

Breno Schmidt and Robert Tumarkin
UNSW




                                    1
 Overview




    Objects
    Basic types
    Assignment statements
    Names
    Downloading stock prices




                               2
 Important




        This week will introduce some concepts that will be explored in
          greater depth later in the course. I’ll point these out as we
                                    progress.




                                                                          3
 Objects
 Review: Important concepts in Python




 The following are fundamental concepts in Python
     A class is a blueprint that defines the structure and behaviour of its instances. Classes
     define:
        Attributes: The data (or information) stored in its objects
        Methods: The actions or behaviours its objects can perform




                                                                                                 4
 Review: Important concepts in Python




 The following are fundamental concepts in Python
     A class is a blueprint that defines the structure and behaviour of its instances. Classes
     define:
        Attributes: The data (or information) stored in its objects
        Methods: The actions or behaviours its objects can perform
     An instance is a specific object created from a class.
        Each instance shares the same behaviour as the class but can hold its own data.




                                                                                                 4
 Review: Important concepts in Python




 The following are fundamental concepts in Python
     A class is a blueprint that defines the structure and behaviour of its instances. Classes
     define:
        Attributes: The data (or information) stored in its objects
        Methods: The actions or behaviours its objects can perform
     An instance is a specific object created from a class.
        Each instance shares the same behaviour as the class but can hold its own data.
     An object is an instance of a class stored in computer memory




                                                                                                 4
 Review: Important concepts in Python




 The following are fundamental concepts in Python
     A class is a blueprint that defines the structure and behaviour of its instances. Classes
     define:
        Attributes: The data (or information) stored in its objects
        Methods: The actions or behaviours its objects can perform
     An instance is a specific object created from a class.
        Each instance shares the same behaviour as the class but can hold its own data.
     An object is an instance of a class stored in computer memory

                In this course, we’ll use the terms object and instance interchangeably.




                                                                                                 4
 Python: Creating instances with constructors and
literals
 Review: Objects in Python




      Python is an object-oriented language
          Everything in Python is an object (an instance of some class)
      A class is a blueprint for objects
          Calling a class constructs an instance
          The class defines the attributes and methods shared by its instances
      Every instance has exactly one class
          That class is the instance’s type
 For our purposes, an instance’s type is simply its class (the one you called).




                                                                                  5
 Python: Creating instances with constructors and literals




 Python uses the class as a template to create instances.

 You can always create an instance with the class name and parameter values in parentheses.
 Examples include:
      int(1), for integers
      float(1.0), for non-integer real numbers
      str('This is text'), for text


                                     This approach uses constructors.




                                                                                              6
 Python: Creating instances with constructors and literals




 Python uses the class as a template to create instances.

 Fundamental classes allow you to create instances with a shorthand that requires just
 specifying a value.
 For example,
      1 (creates the same object as int(1))
      1.0 (creates the same object as float(1.0))
      'This is text' (creates the same object as str('This is text'))


                                        This approach uses literals.




                                                                                         7
 Python: Creating instances with constructors and literals




 Why use literals?

      They are easier to read, cleaner, and shorter
      Python optimises literals so they are typically faster than constructors


 Why use constructors?

      Most of the time, you must. Every class has a constructor, while only a select few classes
      have literals.
      The resulting class is very explicit.
      They are not susceptible to minor typographical errors (e.g., whole number integers and
      floating points)



                     In general, use a literal instead of the constructor whenever possible.




                                                                                                   8
 Python: Basic building blocks
 Basic building blocks




 Five of Python’s essential types:

 Four primitive types

      int: Integers representing whole numbers exactly
      float: Floating points representing real numbers inexactly
      str: Strings of characters representing textual information
      bool: Boolean values representing logical True/False

 One special ‘singleton’ type

      NoneType: None, a single instance representing missing information




                                                                           9
 Whole numbers: Integer




 Integers are whole-valued numerical data (positive, zero, and negative).

 Instances are created by:
      A sequence of digits without a decimal point:
          Python recognises 1 as an integer.
          Python considers 1. and 1.0 to be floating-point numbers.
      A preceding negative sign indicates a negative value:
      Underscores may be used to group digits:
          Python recognises 1_000 as an integer
      Commas may not be used to group digits:
          Python interprets 1,000 to be a tuple (to be discussed later)

                            Integers are represented as instances of the int class




                                                                                     10
 Real numbers: Floating point




 Floating point numbers (floats) are inexact, binary representations of real-valued numbers.

 Instances are created by:
      Two methods:
          A sequence of digits followed by a decimal point with optional additional digits after the decimal
             point.
               • Python considers 1. and 1.0 to be floating-point numbers.
               • Python considers 1.1 to be a floating-point number.
            A decimal point followed by digits.
      A preceding negative sign indicates a negative value:
      Underscores may be used to group digits:
          Python recognises 1_000.1 as an integer
      Commas may not be used to group digits:
          Python interprets 1,000.1 to be a tuple (to be discussed later)

                   Floating point numbers are represented as instances of the float class




                                                                                                                11
 Textual information: String



 Strings are textual data.

 Instances are created by:
      Text on a single line surrounded by single- or double-quotes.
      Text on a single or multiple line surrounded by three repeated single- or double-quotes.
      Use these only when appropriate.
      The symbol used to start the string must be used to end it.
 Examples:
     string_a = "This is a my favourite sentence."
     string_b = 'This is a my favourite sentence.'
     string_c = """This is my favourite sentence. I have so much to say
                   that my thoughts go on to a second line. Let me tell
                   you about the time ..."""
     string_d = '''This is my favourite sentence. I have so much to say
                   that my thoughts go on to a second line. Let me tell
                   you about the time ...'''



                             Strings are represented as instances of the str class



                                                                                                 12
 Textual information: String




 Note that quotation characters can be included by either:

      Starting the string with a different quotation character, e.g., starting a string with
      double-quotes, will let you have single-quotes inside it.
     "When Cleese says: 'Now that's what I call a dead parrot'"


      Preceding (escaping) the quotation character with a backslash: \" or \'
     "Narrator: \"And now for something completely different \""
     'Narrator: \'And now for something completely different \''




                                                                                               13
 True/False information: Boolean




 Booleans are the logical values of true and false.

 The two instances are created by:
      True
      False

 Capitalisation matters. Python considers lowercase true and false to be variable names.


                        True and False are the only instances of the bool class




                                                                                           14
 Missing information: None




 NoneType is used to represent missing information.

 The single instance is created by:
      The keyword None
 Capitalisation matters. Python considers none to be a variable name.


                        None is the only member of Python’s NoneType class




                                                                             15
 Missing information: None




 None is very useful because information is often missing. Note that:

      Operations between None and other basic types are not permitted.
      Method calls with None will yield an error.


 None means that something is missing. It does not mean 0, False, or an empty string, which are
                                   instances of their respective types.




                                                                                                  16
 Key takeaways




    Creating instances
       Constructors: ClassName(...) (always available).
       Literals: shorthand for fundamental classes (1, 1.0, 'text', True, None).
    Essential types
       int: Represent whole numbers
       float: Represent real numbers with a decimal point; inexact representation.
       str: Represent text in quotes ("..." or '...'; triple quotes for multi-line).
       bool: Represent exactly True or False (capitalisation matters)
       NoneType: None, a single value meaning missing information
    Pitfalls to remember
       None is not 0, False, or ""; don’t do arithmetic or method calls on None
       1 is an int, while 1.0 and 1. are float


                                                                                        17
 Identifiers and names
 Once we have created an object in Python, we need a way to identify, refer to, and
                                     use it.




                                                                                     18
 Identifiers and names




 Names are an imperfect way to identify instances.

      Instances may have multiple names: Germany (an instance of a Country) is also known
      as Deutschland (German), Allemagne (French), Alemania (Spanish), Saksa (Finnish),
      among others.
      Instances may change names: This School (an instance of a University division) has been
      known as the Faculty of Commerce (1955-1987), the Faculty of Commerce and Economics
      (1988-2005), the Faculty of Business (2006), the Australian School of Business
      (2007-2013), and UNSW Business School (2014 - present).
      Names can change instances: The Prime Minister of Australia (a name) has been
      Anthony Albanese (2022–present), Scott Morrison (2018–2022), Malcolm Turnbull
      (2015–2018), Tony Abbott (2013–2015), Julia Gillard (2010–2013), Kevin Rudd
      (2007–2010; 2013), John Howard (1996–2007), Paul Keating (1991–1996), Bob Hawke
      (1983–1991), and others.




                                                                                                19
 Identifiers and names




 Names are an imperfect way to identify instances.

      Instances may have multiple names: Germany (an instance of a Country) is also known
      as Deutschland (German), Allemagne (French), Alemania (Spanish), Saksa (Finnish),
      among others.
      Instances may change names: This School (an instance of a University division) has been
      known as the Faculty of Commerce (1955-1987), the Faculty of Commerce and Economics
      (1988-2005), the Faculty of Business (2006), the Australian School of Business
      (2007-2013), and UNSW Business School (2014 - present).
      Names can change instances: The Prime Minister of Australia (a name) has been
      Anthony Albanese (2022–present), Scott Morrison (2018–2022), Malcolm Turnbull
      (2015–2018), Tony Abbott (2013–2015), Julia Gillard (2010–2013), Kevin Rudd
      (2007–2010; 2013), John Howard (1996–2007), Paul Keating (1991–1996), Bob Hawke
      (1983–1991), and others.


                          We need unique and permanent identifiers for instances.




                                                                                                19
 Identifiers and names




 In Python, a name is a references to an object stored in memory.

                                               name   Ñ obj

                   We use names (variables) to refer to objects in the computer memory.




                                                                                          20
 Identifiers and names




 Names reflects real-world scenarios. . .

      Instances can have multiple names:
         The lecturers_phone is also that_annoying_persons_phone.
      Instances can change names:
         The neighbours_device becomes my_backup_device if your neighbour gives it to you.
      Names can change instances:
         This lecturers_phone is different from the lecturers_phone for a different class.
                                 But names do not uniquely identify objects.




                                                                                              21
 Identifiers and names




 To identify an object uniquely, we need to examine its “identity”:

      The identity of an object is an integer which is guaranteed to be unique and constant for
      this object during its lifetime.
      It is assigned by the Python interpreter when the object is created and remains constant
      until the object is deleted.




                                                                                                  22
 Identifiers and names




 To identify an object uniquely, we need to examine its “identity”:

      The identity of an object is an integer which is guaranteed to be unique and constant for
      this object during its lifetime.
      It is assigned by the Python interpreter when the object is created and remains constant
      until the object is deleted.


               At a given point in time, two objects have the same identify if,
                                    and only if, they are the same object.




                                                                                                  22
 Identifiers and names




 We can access the identifier for each object using Python’s id(object) function.

 In an interactive Python environment, we can explore the ids of different instances:
     >>> id(1)
     4366313776
     >>> id(True)
     4376937664



 Note: The identifiers you obtain from running the code will differ from those above.




                                                                                        23
 Identifiers and names




 When executing code:

      Python will give each instance a unique identifier
      Python will keep this identifier constant as long as the instance exists


 This gives us two important takeaways:
   1. We generally shouldn’t use identifiers in our code. Identifiers will differ across computers
      and can even differ on a single computer each time Python is executed.
   2. We can rely on the Python interpreter to give each instance a unique and constant
      identifier.




                                                                                                     24
 Identifiers and names




 We use names as a way to refer to objects:

                                              name   Ñ obj




                                                             25
 Identifiers and names




 We use names as a way to refer to objects:

                                              name   Ñ obj

 Viewing names as links to unique identifiers reflects real-world scenarios:
      Instances can have multiple names:
          The lecturers_phone Ñ 123456 is also that_annoying_persons_phone Ñ 123456.
      Instances can change names:
          The neighbours_device Ñ 867137 becomes my_backup_device Ñ 867137 if your neighbour
           gives it to you.
      Names can change instances:
          The lecturers_phone Ñ 123456 will become lecturers_phone Ñ 612584 once you attend a
           different class.




                                                                                                 25
 Identifiers and names




 We use names as a way to refer to objects:

                                                name   Ñ obj
 Suppose we have two names x and y:

      If id(x) is the same as id(y), then x and y are two names for the same object:
                                                 x   Ñ obj Ð y
      If id(x) is not the same as id(y), then x and y are names for different objects:
                                      x   Ñ obj1, y Ñ obj2, obj1  obj2




                                                                                         26
 Identifiers and names




 Recall:

           At a given point in time, two objects have the same identify if,
                                and only if, they are the same object.




                                                                              27
 Identifiers and names




 Recall:

             At a given point in time, two objects have the same identify if,
                                  and only if, they are the same object.



 We can test if two names refer to the same object using:
      The id() function and boolean equality == (note the double equals sign).
                                         id(x) == id(y) -> bool

      The is operator:
                                                   x is y


             A value of True means that the names refer to the same object,
                                a value of False means they do not.




                                                                                 27
 Key takeaways




    Names are imperfect ways to refer to instances.
    The identifier concept ensures that every instance of every class has a unique identity.
    Names are then a way to refer to an identifier.
       The name of an instance may change – its identifier will not.
       A name may change the instance to which it relates.
    We can write the link as name     Ñ obj




                                                                                               28
 Assignment statements
 Once we have created an object in Python, we need a way to identify, refer to, and
                                     use it.




                                                                                     29
 Assignment statements




 We will break the discussion of assignments into a few sub-sections:

      Variables and assignment statements
      Expressions
      Variable naming




                                                                        30
 Python: Variables and assignment statements
 Python: Variables and assignment statements




 When Python creates an object:

      It stores the object somewhere in the computer’s memory.
      The location of the object is called its memory address.
 Python can find objects using their memory addresses, but:

      Programmers do not know these addresses in advance.
      Addresses can change each time we run the program.

                          It is hard to use memory addresses directly in code. . .




                                                                                     31
 Python: Variables and assignment statements




 A variable is a name in our code that Python links to an object’s memory address while it executes a
 program.
                                               name   Ñ obj
      We use variables to store and access information.
      This information can be an int, str, or any Python object.
      The object can be used or changed by our code.




                                                                                                        32
 Python: Variables and assignment statements




 We attach a variable to a memory location using an assignment statement

 Assignment statements have the form:
                                    variable_name = expression

 On execution, Python:
   1. Checks that variable_name is a valid name
   2. Evaluates the expression
   3. Assigns the instance produced by the expression to variable_name.




                                                                           33
 Python: Variables and assignment statements




 The use of the equals sign in the assignment statement may be confusing to new programmers.
                                     variable_name = expression


      Read the statement as variable_name is assigned to the result of the expression.
      Do not interpret this as variable_name is equal to the expression.


                             We will return to this point later in the lecture.




                                                                                               34
 Python: Variables and assignment statements




 Basic assignment statement using the primitive types and None are shown below:

     x = 5                # int
     pi_approx = 3.14     # float
     name = "Alice"       # str
     is_valid = True      # bool
     result = None        # NoneType




                                                                                  35
 Python: Variables and assignment statements




 Let’s pause and summarise what we’ve learned:

      Identifiers and names:
         Names are imperfect ways to refer to instances.
         The identifier concept ensures that every instance of every class has a unique identity.
         Names are then a way to refer to an identifier.
             • The name of an instance may change – its identifier will not.
             • A name may change the instance to which it relates.
           We can write the link as name Ñ
                                          obj

     Variables and assignment statements:
         A variable is a name in our code that Python will link with an instance’s unique identifier as it
          executes a program.
         We attach a variable to a memory location using an assignment statement: variable_name =
            expression




                                                                                                              36
 Python: Expressions
 Python: Expressions




 An expression is a combination of instances, operations, and functions. Python evaluates expressions,
 producing an instance as the result.

 Some expressions using integers are:
      int(1)

      int(1) + int(2)

      abs(int(-2))

      int(9) ** abs(int(-2))




                                                                                                         37
 Python: Expressions




 An expression is a combination of instances, operations, and functions. Python evaluates expressions,
 producing an instance as the result.

 Some expressions using integers are:
      int(1)

      int(1) + int(2)

      abs(int(-2))

      int(9) ** abs(int(-2))



    The examples in this section use int(), float(), and str() constructors instead of literals to draw
  attention to the fact that Python must create an instance of any object before it can perform any work.




                                                                                                            37
 Python: Expressions




 Python evaluates expressions by explicitly working with instances.

 When evaluating int(1), Python will:
      Create an int instance with a value of 1


                      An int instance with a value of 1 is the result of this expression.




                                                                                            38
 Python: Expressions




 Python evaluates expressions by explicitly working with instances.

 When evaluating int(1) + int(2), Python will:
      Create an int instance with a value of 1
      Create an int instance with a value of 2
      Verify that the int class supports addition
      Create an int instance with a value of 1 plus 2


                      An int instance with a value of 3 is the result of this expression.




                                                                                            39
 Python: Expressions




 Python evaluates expressions by explicitly working with instances.

 When evaluating abs(int(-2)), Python will:
      Create an int instance with a value of -2
      Verify that the int class supports the abs absolute value function
      Create an int instance with a value of the absolute value of -2


                      An int instance with a value of 2 is the result of this expression.




                                                                                            40
 Python: Expressions




 Python evaluates expressions by explicitly working with instances.

 When evaluating int(9) ** abs(int(-2)), Python will:
      Create an int instance with a value of 9
      Evaluate the abs(int(-2)) sub-expression
          Create an int instance with a value of -2
          Verify that the int class supports the abs absolute value function
          Create an int instance with a value of 2
      Verify that the int class supports exponentiation using the ** operator.
      Create an int instance with the value of 9 raised to the 2nd power.


                     An int instance with a value of 81 is the result of this expression.




                                                                                            41
 Python: Expressions




 Python evaluates expressions by explicitly working with instances.

 When evaluating float(9) ** abs(int(-2)), Python will:
      Create a float instance with a value of 9
      Evaluate the abs(int(-2)) sub-expression
          Create an int instance with a value of -2
          Verify that the int class supports the abs absolute value function
          Create an int instance with a value of 2
      Verify that the float class supports exponentiation to an int power using the ** operator.
      Create a float instance with the value of 9 raised to the 2nd power.


                    A float instance with a value of 81.0 is the result of this expression.




                                                                                                   42
 Python: Expressions




 A note about mathematical operators for integers and floating-point instances


 Most algebraic operators work as expected:
      Addition: + (e.g., x + y)
      Subtraction: - (e.g., x - y)
      Multiplication * (e.g., x * y)
      Division: / (e.g., x / y)

 One common algebraic operator works differently than expected:
      Exponentiation: **


 Python uses two stars (**) for exponentiation. The caret operator (ˆ) has an entirely different meaning.




                                                                                                            43
 Python: Expressions




 Python evaluates expressions by explicitly working with instances.

     "Hello" .upper()


 When evaluating "Hello".upper(), Python will:
      Create a str instance with a “Hello”
      Call the instance method .upper on this instance
      Receive a new instance from the method with all letters in uppercase


                  A str instance with a value of “HELLO” is the result of this expression.




                                                                                             44
 Python: Expressions




 Python evaluates expressions by explicitly working with instances.

     (True or False) and False


 When evaluating (True or False) and False, Python will:
      Process the parenthetical phrase
         Create a bool instance with the value of True
         Create a bool instance with the value of False
         Apply the or operator on the previous two instances, yielding True
      Finalise the expression
         Use True as the result of the parenthetical phrase
         Recall the bool instance with the value of False
         Apply the and operator on the previous two instances, yielding False

                       A bool instance with a value of False result of this expression.




                                                                                          45
 Key takeaways




    Identifiers and names:
       Names are imperfect ways to refer to instances.
       The identifier concept ensures that every instance of every class has a unique identity.
       Names are then a way to refer to an identifier.
           • The name of an instance may change – its identifier will not.
           • A name may change the instance to which it relates.
         We can write the link as name Ñ
                                        obj

    Variables and assignment statements:
       A variable is a name in our code that Python will link with an instance’s unique identifier as it
        executes a program.
       We attach a variable to a memory location using an assignment statement: variable_name =
          expression

    Expressions:
       An expression is a combination of instances, operations, and functions.
       Python evaluates expressions by explicitly working with instances, producing an instance as the
          result.




                                                                                                            46
 Python: An aside about rules and conventions
 Rules and conventions




 Rules and conventions are important to follow when writing code:

      Rules: Define what you are allowed to do
      Conventions: Define what you should do and, more importantly, what others expect you
      to have done.


 The Python community treats conventions seriously.

     “PEP 8 - Style Guide for Python Code” is an essential reference for Python coding
     conventions.




                                                                                             47
 A consistent, disciplined approach that follows established rules and conventions will
         help you and others understand and maintain your code effectively.




                                                                                         48
 Python: Variable names
 Rules: Naming




 Naming rules apply to any variable or function we create.

      Names must start with a letter or underscore (“_“).
      Names can only contain alphanumeric characters and underscores.
      Names are case-sensitive.


           As long as you follow these rules, you can use nearly anything as a name, however. . .




                                                                                                    49
 Python: Variable names




 The Python community has settled on snake_case as the de facto standard when naming variables:

      Variable names are written in lower-case
      Words are separated with underscores




                                                                                                  50
 Python: Variable names




 The Python community has settled on snake_case as the de facto standard when naming variables:

      Variable names are written in lower-case
      Words are separated with underscores


             It is best to be expressive when naming variables, clearly describing
                                the information to which the variable relates.


 For example, when naming a variable reflecting the price of a call option:
      Best: call_option_price
      Bad: calloptionprice
      Worse: cop




                                                                                                  50
 Python: Variable names - Rule (Reserved keywords)




 Python reserved keywords cannot be used for variable or function names. These include:

 False       await        else         import       pass
 None        break        except       in           raise
 True        class        finally      is           return
 and         continue     for          lambda       try
 as          def          from         nonlocal     while
 assert      del          global       not          with
 async       elif         if           or           yield


                   Using a reserved keyword as a variable name results in a syntax error.




                                                                                            51
 Python: Variable names - Convention (Built-in function names)


 Python built-in function names should not be used for variable or function names. These include:
     abs              enumerate       list             slice
     aiter            eval            locals           sorted
     all              exec            map              staticmethod
     anext            filter          max              str
     any              float           memoryview       sum
     ascii            format          min              super
     bin              frozenset       next             tuple
     bool             getattr         object           type
     breakpoint       globals         oct              vars
     bytearray        hasattr         open             zip
     bytes            hash            ord
     callable         help            pow
     chr              hex             print
     classmethod      id              property
     compile          input           range
     complex          int             repr
     delattr          isinstance      reversed
     dict             issubclass      round
     dir              iter            set
     divmod           len             setattr



  Using a built-in function name as a variable name can be confusing and may lead to unexpected errors.


                                                                                                          52
 Python: Variable names - Convention (Single leading underscores)




 Python programmers typically use a single leading underscore to indicate that a variable or function should
 be treated as private.

      A leading underscore name starts with an underscore: _var or _method.
      Private variables and methods are “internal” to a module. They are not intended for users
      of a module.
      Using a leading underscore to indicate something is private is only a convention; it is not
      part of the language’s syntax.


   You should only use a leading underscore when you intend to treat the variable or method as private.




                                                                                                               53
 Python: Variable names - Rule (Double leading underscores)




 The Python interpreter treats variables beginning with a double underscore specially.

      Names with leading double underscores, like __var or __method, will be mangled in some
      cases. This changes their names internally so they are hard to access within the program.
      Other names like __file__ or __init__ have special meaning and follow a “magic name”
      convention.


                Avoid using double underscores unless you clearly understand how they work.




                                                                                                  54
 Python: Variable names - Best practices




 Combining “PEP 8 - Style Guide for Python Code” with the previous rules suggests

      Variables and methods should be written in “snake case” – lower-case letters with words
      separated by underscores: e.g., my_standard_variable (PEP 8)
      Constant variables (variables that do not change as a program executes) should be written
      in “screaming snake case” – capitalised letters throughout with words separated by
      underscores: e.g., MY_CONSTANT_VARIABLE (PEP 8)
      Do not use reserved keywords or built-in function names for variables.
      Do not start a variable name with a single underscore unless it is intended to be private.
      Never start a variable name with two underscores.




                                                                                                   55
 Key takeaways




    Name regular variables in snake_case
    Name constant variables in SCREAMING_SNAKE_CASE
    Do not use reserved keywords of built-in function names
    Limit the use of single leading underscores and double leading underscores




                                                                                 56
 Python: An aside about rules and conventions
 Rules and conventions




 Rules and conventions are important to follow when writing code:

      Rules: Define what you are allowed to do
      Conventions: Define what you should do and, more importantly, what others expect you
      to have done.


 The Python community treats conventions seriously.

     “PEP 8 - Style Guide for Python Code” is an essential reference for Python coding
     conventions.




                                                                                             57
 A consistent, disciplined approach that follows established rules and conventions will
         help you and others understand and maintain your code effectively.




                                                                                         58
 Python: Variable names
 Rules: Naming




 Naming rules apply to any variable or function we create.

      Names must start with a letter or underscore (“_“).
      Names can only contain alphanumeric characters and underscores.
      Names are case-sensitive.


           As long as you follow these rules, you can use nearly anything as a name, however. . .




                                                                                                    59
 Python: Variable names




 The Python community has settled on snake_case as the de facto standard when naming variables:

      Variable names are written in lower-case
      Words are separated with underscores




                                                                                                  60
 Python: Variable names




 The Python community has settled on snake_case as the de facto standard when naming variables:

      Variable names are written in lower-case
      Words are separated with underscores


             It is best to be expressive when naming variables, clearly describing
                                the information to which the variable relates.


 For example, when naming a variable reflecting the price of a call option:
      Best: call_option_price
      Bad: calloptionprice
      Worse: cop




                                                                                                  60
 Python: Variable names - Rule (Reserved keywords)




 Python reserved keywords cannot be used for variable or function names. These include:

 False       await        else         import       pass
 None        break        except       in           raise
 True        class        finally      is           return
 and         continue     for          lambda       try
 as          def          from         nonlocal     while
 assert      del          global       not          with
 async       elif         if           or           yield


                   Using a reserved keyword as a variable name results in a syntax error.




                                                                                            61
 Python: Variable names - Convention (Built-in function names)


 Python built-in function names should not be used for variable or function names. These include:
     abs              enumerate       list             slice
     aiter            eval            locals           sorted
     all              exec            map              staticmethod
     anext            filter          max              str
     any              float           memoryview       sum
     ascii            format          min              super
     bin              frozenset       next             tuple
     bool             getattr         object           type
     breakpoint       globals         oct              vars
     bytearray        hasattr         open             zip
     bytes            hash            ord
     callable         help            pow
     chr              hex             print
     classmethod      id              property
     compile          input           range
     complex          int             repr
     delattr          isinstance      reversed
     dict             issubclass      round
     dir              iter            set
     divmod           len             setattr



  Using a built-in function name as a variable name can be confusing and may lead to unexpected errors.


                                                                                                          62
 Python: Variable names - Convention (Single leading underscores)




 Python programmers typically use a single leading underscore to indicate that a variable or function should
 be treated as private.

      A leading underscore name starts with an underscore: _var or _method.
      Private variables and methods are “internal” to a module. They are not intended for users
      of a module.
      Using a leading underscore to indicate something is private is only a convention; it is not
      part of the language’s syntax.


   You should only use a leading underscore when you intend to treat the variable or method as private.




                                                                                                               63
 Python: Variable names - Rule (Double leading underscores)




 The Python interpreter treats variables beginning with a double underscore specially.

      Names with leading double underscores, like __var or __method, will be mangled in some
      cases. This changes their names internally so they are hard to access within the program.
      Other names like __file__ or __init__ have special meaning and follow a “magic name”
      convention.


                Avoid using double underscores unless you clearly understand how they work.




                                                                                                  64
 Python: Variable names - Best practices




 Combining “PEP 8 - Style Guide for Python Code” with the previous rules suggests

      Variables and methods should be written in “snake case” – lower-case letters with words
      separated by underscores: e.g., my_standard_variable (PEP 8)
      Constant variables (variables that do not change as a program executes) should be written
      in “screaming snake case” – capitalised letters throughout with words separated by
      underscores: e.g., MY_CONSTANT_VARIABLE (PEP 8)
      Do not use reserved keywords or built-in function names for variables.
      Do not start a variable name with a single underscore unless it is intended to be private.
      Never start a variable name with two underscores.




                                                                                                   65
 Key takeaways




    Name regular variables in snake_case
    Name constant variables in SCREAMING_SNAKE_CASE
    Do not use reserved keywords of built-in function names
    Limit the use of single leading underscores and double leading underscores

     In Python, everything is an object

                                                                          67
 Objects, names, and ID



     In Python, everything is an object
        Python works by creating and manipulating objects
     Objects are instances stored in computer memory
     We do not access these objects directly
        To use an object, we must first give it a name
     The Python interpreter creates (and names) many objects by default
        These are built-in objects, such as the print function
     How to check if two objects are the same
        id(obj) -> int: Unique identifier for the object
        obj1 is obj2: True iff both objects are the same
     How can I check if two objects have the same value?
        obj1 == obj2: True if same value (not necessarily same object)
     Example:
        >>> 1 is 1.0    # -> False
        >>> 1 == 1.0    # -> True



                                                                          67
 Summary: Assignment statements



    A statement is an instruction that the Python interpreter can execute.
       In Python, a statement is the smallest standalone unit of execution
       Statements often contain expressions
       An expression is any code that produces a value (by creating, manipulating, or returning objects)
    Assignment statement have the form
                                        <target> = <expression>
       <target> is often a variable name
           • In that case, the result of <expression> is assigned to <target>

    Evaluation steps:
      1. Check if <target> is valid (e.g., a valid name)
      2. Evaluate <expression>, which produces an object
      3. Assign the object produced to <target>


                                       Let’s look at an example




                                                                                                            68
 Example: A simple statement




 Consider the statement x    1        2

     x = 1 + 2 is a valid Python statement

     The interpreter evaluates this statement as follows:
       1. Check if x is a valid name
       2. Evaluate the expression 1        2 step by step:
           2.1 The literal 1:
               Python creates an instance of the class int with value 1.
           2.2 The operator +:
               Python interprets this as an instruction to add two int instances.
           2.3 The literal 2:
               Python creates another int instance with value 2.
           2.4 Performing the addition:
               The class int defines how + works for its instances.
               Python adds 1 and 2, producing a new int instance with value 3.
       3. Assign the result (int: 3) to the variable x
